message:
test(fsqlite-core): pin fresh-bead keyed lookup; modernize harness waker; add perf bin

Three small, independently useful additions grouped into one commit
because they touch the same corner of the crate graph and share no
production-code changes.

Fresh-bead keyed lookup regression test
---------------------------------------
`crates/fsqlite-core/tests/beads_rebuild_fresh_lookup.rs` is a new
integration test that proves — directly on the `Connection` API,
without going through beads_rust or any higher-layer storage wrapper —
that freshly-inserted rows on a BEADS-style `issues` table are visible
to `query_with_params`, `query_row_with_params`, and full-scan
`query` immediately after INSERT.

The test models the exact failure mode that beads_rust was working
around with CTE wrappers under issues #226/#229/#252/#254/#255/#256:
create a BEADS-shaped `issues` table (TEXT PRIMARY KEY `id`,
`content_hash`, `title`, `status`, `priority`, timestamps), insert a
batch of fresh rows back-to-back, and assert that:

- full-scan `SELECT id FROM issues ORDER BY rowid` returns every ID,
- keyed `SELECT id FROM issues WHERE id = ?` returns exactly the
  same ID via both `query_with_params` (row list) and
  `query_row_with_params` (single row), and
- `query_row_with_params` returns `QueryReturnedNoRows` — not a
  silent empty row-set — when the requested ID is truly absent.

With this test in place on the fsqlite side, beads_rust can drop its
CTE/full-scan workarounds and rely on the plain keyed lookup; the
paired `refactor(storage): drop fsqlite-planner workarounds` commit
over there does exactly that.

`Waker::noop()` harness modernization
-------------------------------------
`commit_pipeline.rs` and `test_e2e_commit_pipeline_cancel_safety.rs`
both defined a local `struct NoopWaker` implementing `std::task::Wake`,
then wrapped it in `Arc::new(NoopWaker)` and fed it to
`Waker::from(...)` to drive a manual `block_on` loop for cancel-safety
testing. Rust now exposes `std::task::Waker::noop()` as a stable
`&'static Waker`, which gives us the same zero-wake waker without the
`Arc<dyn Wake>` boilerplate.

Replace the local struct with `let waker = Waker::noop();` and feed
it to `Context::from_waker(waker)` — in the cancel-safety test this
also lets us drop the `std::sync::Arc` import, which is otherwise
unused. No change in test behaviour.

`perf-update-delete` profiling binary
-------------------------------------
`crates/fsqlite-e2e/src/bin/perf_update_delete.rs` is a narrow
profiling binary that runs the UPDATE/DELETE workload from
`comprehensive-bench`'s Section 6 without the C SQLite comparison or
the full benchmarking harness, so `perf record` / flamegraph stacks
stay focused on the fsqlite engine instead of being diluted by
comparison bookkeeping.

CLI is positional and defaults to "both, 10k rows, 10 iters":
```
perf-update-delete [rows] [iters] [which]   # which ∈ update|delete|both
```

The union-view-root-page collision test picks up an unrelated
`cargo fmt` pass in the same file (line joins, argument layout)
because the formatter touched it alongside the surrounding work; no
behavioural change.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
